elements.sent-2411-024: It explains several gravitational anomalies observed at different scales: the flatness of rotation curves of spiral galaxies, the dynamics of galaxy clusters, the distribution of large-scale structures in the Universe, and the anisotropies in the temperature of the cosmic microwave background.
elements.sent-2411-025: Precise measurements of the latter, possibly combined with other techniques, show that DM constitutes about a quarter of the Universe's energy budget.
elements.sent-2411-026: Although we have reliable observational evidence of DM's existence, its nature remains a mystery, as no observation has yet shown that DM can interact with ordinary matter other than gravitationally.
elements.sent-2411-027: Numerous hypotheses about its nature remain.
elements.sent-2411-028: DM could exist as elementary particles not included in the Standard Model of particle physics, or as macroscopic compact objects such as primordial black holes (PBH).
elements.sent-2411-029: To reveal the nature of DM, or to rule out hypotheses concerning it, several observational techniques are available.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this thesis, we focus on the method of indirect detection, which involves looking for signals of the annihilation or decay of DM in the form of charged cosmic rays, photons or neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-031: Each product carries different types of information.
elements.sent-2411-032: Photons and neutrinos, being neutral particles, can propagate without being deflected by the surrounding magnetic fields, making it easier to trace their source of emission.
elements.sent-2411-033: Charged cosmic rays, on the other hand, may consist of antimatter, which is less likely produced by astrophysical processes and can therefore be detected with a low background.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this thesis, we study the emission of secondary photons by the interaction of DM products with the Galactic environment.
elements.sent-2411-035: Specifically, we consider the case in which DM is a particle with a mass below a GeV.
elements.sent-2411-036: The electrons and positrons produced could interact with ambient photons in the Galaxy, producing $X$-rays through inverse Compton scattering.
elements.sent-2411-037: The prediction of the spectrum of this radiation, compared with data from $X$-ray observatories, provides strong constraints on this type of DM.
elements.sent-2411-038: Similarly, we apply this same principle to the case of PBH evaporation in order to impose strong constraints on them.
elements.sent-2411-039: Elle explique plusieurs anomalies gravitationnelles pr\'esentes dans des syst\`emes \`a diff\'erentes \'echelles : la platitude des courbes de rotation des galaxies spirales, la dynamique des amas de galaxies, la distribution des structures \`a grande \'echelle dans l'Univers et les anisotropies de la temp\'erature du fond diffus cosmologique.
elements.sent-2411-040: Des mesures pr\'ecises de ces derni\`eres, \'eventuellement combin\'ees \`a d'autres techniques, montrent que la MN constitue environ un quart de l'\'energie totale de l'Univers.
elements.sent-2411-041: Bien que nous ayons des preuves observationnelles fiables de l'existence de la MN, sa nature reste un myst\`ere, car \`a ce jour, aucune observation n'a pu montrer que la MN peut interagir avec la mati\`ere ordinaire autrement que gravitationnellement.
elements.sent-2411-042: De nombreuses hypoth\`eses sur sa nature subsistent.
elements.sent-2411-043: La MN pourrait exister sous la forme de particules \'el\'ementaires qui ne font pas partie du Mod\`ele Standard de la physique des particules, ou sous forme d'objets macroscopiques compacts tels que les trous noirs primordiaux (TNP).
elements.sent-2411-044: Pour r\'ev\'eler la nature de la MN, ou \`a d\'efaut, pour \'ecarter des hypoth\`eses la concernant, plusieurs techniques d'observation existent.
elements.sent-2411-045: Dans cette th\`ese, nous nous concentrons sur la m\'ethode de d\'etection indirecte, qui consiste \`a rechercher des signaux d'annihilation ou de d\'esint\'egration de la MN sous forme de rayons cosmiques charg\'es, de photons ou de neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-046: Chaque produit porte diff\'erents types d'informations.
elements.sent-2411-047: Les photons et les neutrinos, \'etant des particules neutres, peuvent se propager sans \^etre d\'evi\'es par les champs magn\'etiques environnants, ce qui facilite la localisation de leur source d'\'emission.
elements.sent-2411-048: Les rayons cosmiques charg\'es, en revanche, peuvent \^etre constitu\'es d'antimati\`ere, qui est tr\`es peu produite par des processus astrophysiques et peut donc \^etre d\'etect\'ee avec un faible signal de fond.
elements.sent-2411-049: Dans cette th\`ese, nous \'etudions l'\'emission de photons secondaires par l'interaction des produits issus de la MN avec le milieu Galactique.
elements.sent-2411-050: En particulier, dans le cas o\`u la MN est une particule de masse inf\'erieure \`a un GeV, les \'electrons et positrons produits pourraient interagir avec des photons ambiants de la Galaxie, produisant des rayons $X$ par effet Compton inverse.
elements.sent-2411-051: La pr\'ediction du spectre de ce rayonnement, compar\'ee aux donn\'ees des observatoires \`a rayons $X$, permet d'obtenir de fortes contraintes sur ce type de MN.
elements.sent-2411-052: De m\^eme, nous appliquons ce m\^eme principe au cas de l'\'evaporation des TNP afin de leur imposer de fortes contraintes.
elements.sent-2411-053: So far, we only witness its existence through its gravitational impact in the Universe, such as in the rotation curves of spiral galaxies, the dynamics of galaxy clusters, the distribution of large-scale structures and the temperature anisotropies in the cosmic microwave background (CMB).
elements.sent-2411-054: We also know, thanks to precise measurements of the latter, that DM is not baryonic and constitutes almost 85\% of the total amount of matter contained in the Universe as well as around 25\% of its energy budget.
elements.sent-2411-055: Despite its imposing presence, we know very little about its properties and how it was produced in the early Universe.
elements.sent-2411-056: In turn, its nature remains a mystery and currently there exists a plethora of well-motivated candidates that can describe the observational properties of DM.
elements.sent-2411-057: Experimental methods such as collider searches, direct and indirect detection are crucial to help us understand the big picture, as their role is to test these candidates.
elements.sent-2411-058: All of these aspects -- observational evidence of the existence of DM, possible production scenarios and candidates, and experimental ways to test them -- are explored in Chapter~\ref{chap:DMintro .}
elements.sent-2411-059: In this thesis, we focus on the indirect detection (ID) of DM, which consists of probing signals from DM in the form of stable, charged cosmic rays (such as $e^\pm$ and light antinuclei) or radiation (photons and neutrinos).
elements.sent-2411-060: In particular, if DM is made of unknown particles, their annihilation or decay are expected to produce such signals, which can be hopefully detected in observatories and other experiments.
elements.sent-2411-061: Alternatively, if DM is constituted of macroscopic objects called primordial black holes (PBHs), they can produce similar signals through Hawking evaporation.
elements.sent-2411-062: On the theoretical side, the goal is to predict what is the expected flux of these DM products, which essentially consists of i) computing the spectrum of these products at their production point and ii) dealing with their propagation through the (extra-)galactic medium.
elements.sent-2411-063: These steps depict the interdisciplinary nature of DM ID: the first one is entirely related to particle physics, which predicts what happens between the sole DM annihilation, decay or evaporation and the production of stable states, whereas the second one is related to astrophysics, that predicts what constitutes the interstellar medium through where the DM-produced particles propagate.
elements.sent-2411-064: After computing the expected flux of DM products at Earth, we can compare them with data in order to either provide a DM interpretation of an excess on top of the known astrophysical background, or otherwise set bounds on DM properties.
elements.sent-2411-065: We can conduct this phenomenological approach on various targets such as celestial bodies, our own Galaxy, dwarf spheroidal galaxies and galaxy clusters.
elements.sent-2411-066: The bottom line is that an ideal target for DM ID should be sufficiently rich in DM, to be able to measure a strong signal from it, as well as sufficiently low in terms of astrophysical activity, as its modeling can be challenging and therefore be a source of large uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-067: No target is perfect and compromise has to be made.
elements.sent-2411-068: More details on general DM ID phenomenology are provided in Chapter~\ref{chap:ID .}
elements.sent-2411-069: The phenomenology DM ID is very rich, in the sense that there is a plethora of possible DM candidates that each can provide many types of indirect signals.
elements.sent-2411-070: For example, in Chapter~\ref{chap:subGeV , we consider that DM consists of sub-GeV particles, which is an increasingly attractive assumption, especially due to the lack of signals from paradigmatic GeV$-$TeV DM candidates in experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-071: However, sub-GeV DM can also be challenging to probe indirectly: their annihilation or decay produce sub-GeV $e^\pm$ that are highly impacted by solar winds, decreasing drastically their flux at Earth.
elements.sent-2411-072: Moreover, there is currently no $\gamma$-ray observatory that can probe the $100$ keV $-$ $100$~MeV energy range with a good enough sensitivity, which can limit the detection of photons produced directly by the annihilation or decay of sub-GeV DM.
elements.sent-2411-073: A way to circumvent these issues is to study low-energy photons emitted through final state radiations, radiative decays of DM-produced unstable states but also during the propagation of DM-produced $e^\pm$, mainly through up-scattering ambient photons in the Galaxy via the inverse Compton effect.
elements.sent-2411-074: These photons can then be probed efficiently in $X$-ray observatories.
elements.sent-2411-075: An advantage of sub-GeV DM is that there are only a few kinematically open DM annihilation and decays channels.
elements.sent-2411-076: We considered the following final states: electrons, muons and charged pions.
elements.sent-2411-077: After predicting semi-analytically the flux of $X$-rays for each channel, using a minimalistic propagation setup for DM-produced $e^\pm$, we perform an analysis over various diffuse $X$-rays datasets from {\sc NuStar , {\sc Integral , {\sc Suzaku and {\sc Xmm-Newton .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: We find that the latter provide us among the most stringent constraints on annihilating and decaying sub-GeV DM, due to i) the region where the data have been taken (close to the GC) and ii) how the data were cleaned from point sources.
elements.sent-2411-079: However, our bounds suffer from the uncertainties on the different astrophysical ingredients we used (DM profile, gas and ambient photon densities), whose impact we quantify.
elements.sent-2411-080: In Chapter~\ref{chap:prop , we perform a similar analysis as in Chapter~\ref{chap:subGeV but instead we study the impact on the bounds of adopting a realistic cosmic ray propagation setup.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: In particular, $e^\pm$ produced by lower mass DM can be reaccelerated through their interaction with the turbulent component of the Galactic magnetic field, and would in turn up-scatter ambient photons to $X$-ray energies, which was not the case without this effect.
elements.sent-2411-082: This significantly improves the limits on sub-GeV DM, especially for DM masses below $20$ MeV.
elements.sent-2411-083: For this study, we treat all propagation-related effects using the numerical code \verb|DRAGON2|, and perform computations of the secondary $X$-ray flux using \verb|HERMES|.
elements.sent-2411-084: Unfortunately, the propagation parameters come with their uncertainties as well, and we show how they can impact the robustness of our $X$-ray limits.
elements.sent-2411-085: We also derive complementary bounds on sub-GeV using the local $e^\pm$ flux measured by {\sc Voyager 1 (with the same propagation setup).}
elements.sent-2411-086: They appear to be comparable to other similar limits from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-087: In addition, they are weaker than the $X$-ray ones, although more robust, since they depend less on the choice of the DM profile.
elements.sent-2411-088: PBHs can also constitute a credible DM candidate.
elements.sent-2411-089: These are black holes that are not produced by the collapse of a heavy star, but instead that could appear through the collapse of localised matter over densities in the early Universe.
elements.sent-2411-090: A way to probe these PBHs is to look for the products of their evaporation due to the Hawking mechanism, which has been proven to be efficient at excluding PBHs as a DM component for PBH masses below $5\times10^{17}$~g.
elements.sent-2411-091: The mass range between $\sim 5\times10^{17}$ and $10^{22}$ g is still unconstrained, therefore PBHs constituting the totality of DM remains a possibility as long as their mass is contained in this range.
elements.sent-2411-092: Refining the evaporation limits can therefore help us in probing the lower bound of this mass range, and if nothing is found, constrain it even further.
elements.sent-2411-093: In Chapter~\ref{chap:PBH , we investigate three complementary probes of PBH evaporation.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Since PBHs with a mass above $7.5\times10^{14}$ g are expected to evaporate the same final states as for sub-GeV DM, we evaluate the expected PBH-produced $e^\pm$ flux at Earth as well as the secondary $X$-ray emissions in the same manner as in Chapter~\ref{chap:prop .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Compared to the case of decaying sub-GeV DM, the only thing that changes is the injection spectrum of $e^\pm$ which shows similarities with the one of a black body, convoluted with decay spectrum of evaporated muons and charged pions.
elements.sent-2411-096: The last probe we consider is the expected emission of $511$ keV photons due to both the annihilation of PBH-produced $e^+$ in the interstellar medium and the decay of para-positronium formed by PBH-produced $e^+$ and free $e^-$ in ionised interstellar gas.
elements.sent-2411-097: We then use longitude profiles of the $511$ keV line measured by {\sc Integral in order to set a bound on PBHs.}
elements.sent-2411-098: In the end, we find that the $511$ keV limit is the strongest and most robust one among the three probes, and, in our fiducial scenario of astrophysical parameters, can even compete with other bounds in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-099: Moreover we discuss the impact of different spin and mass distributions on the limits we derive.

elements.chapter-2411-005: Dark matter or Physics' greatest investigation
elements.sent-2411-110: First postulated by Fritz Zwicky in 1933 in his seminal work on the Coma cluster~\cite{Zwicky:1933gu , this idea of an `invisible' type of matter --~that can explain the discrepancy between the cluster's mass computed with the virial theorem -- was however not taken seriously for over 40 years.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Thanks to Vera Rubin and Kent Ford’s work on the measurement of the Andromeda galaxy rotation curve~\cite{Rubin:1970zza , the DM hypothesis was then popularised.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Combination of $X$-ray emission measurements and gravitational lensing on merging galaxy clusters also back up Zwicky's original claim.
elements.sent-2411-113: Finally, the precise measurement of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) by the {\sc Planck experiment~\cite{Planck:2018vyg leads to the same finding at cosmological scales.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Although the existence of DM is a scientific consensus, its nature remains elusive.
elements.sent-2411-115: We have an idea of what could be some of the properties of DM, but many candidates can fit such a description.
elements.sent-2411-116: To this day, many physicists around the globe are committed to build and maintain sophisticated experiments and develop theoretical tools in order to find the culprit.
elements.sent-2411-117: In this chapter, we outline some well-known observational evidence of DM (Section \ref{sec:evidence ), then we enumerate the established properties of DM as well as fitting candidates (Section \ref{sec:DMpropcand ), to finally explain what are some of the current ways to probe them (Section \ref{sec:detecmethods ).}}}
elements.section-2411-001: Evidences of the existence of dark matter
elements.sent-2411-118: Since its original claim in 1933, we have accumulated observational evidence of DM at different scales.
elements.sent-2411-119: This section lists some of the most well-known ones: the discrepancies in rotation curves of spiral galaxies (Section~\ref{subsec:rotcurves ), in the mass of galaxy clusters and evidence from merging ones (Section~\ref{subsec:galclust ) and finally evidence from measurements of temperature anisotropies of the CMB (Section~\ref{subsec:CMB ).}}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Discrepancies in rotation curves of spiral galaxies
elements.sent-2411-120: We begin by computing the circular velocity $v_c$ of stars populating a spiral galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-121: Let us first assume the mass distribution of the spiral galaxy to be spherical.
elements.sent-2411-122: For a given star of mass $m$ in uniform circular motion around the centre of the galaxy at a distance $r$, Newton's second law gives \begin{equation}m\frac{v^2_c(r)}{r} = m\frac{GM(r)}{r^2} \implies v_c(r) = \sqrt{\frac{GM(r)}{r}}\;,\end{equation} where $M(r)=4\pi \int_0^r r'^2\rho(r') dr'$ is the mass enclosed within the radius $r$ and $G$ the gravitational constant (and $\rho(r)$ is the mass density of the galaxy at a radius $r$).
elements.sent-2411-123: In a typical spiral galaxy, all of visible matter is contained in a sphere of radius of $10-20$ kpc, meaning that for larger radii we retrieve the Keplerian behavior $v_c(r) \propto 1/\sqrt{r}$.
elements.sent-2411-124: In a given spiral galaxy, we can measure the circular velocity $v_c$ of visible matter at a distance $r$ from its centre by combining two types of observations: i) for lower $r$, measurements of emission lines in the optical range, ii) for higher $r$, of the $21$ cm line, which is the spectral line created by the spin-flip of the electron of an hydrogen atom.
elements.sent-2411-125: First highlighted by Vera Rubin and Kent Ford in 1970 in the Andromeda galaxy~\cite{Rubin:1970zza , such observations show that $v_c$ is approximately constant at the outskirts of galaxies, and therefore we infer that galaxies are not entirely composed of visible matter: there exists a halo of matter which a priori only interacts gravitationally and which extends beyond the galactic disk (typically $100-200$ kpc from the centre of the galaxy) and satisfies $M(r)\propto r$ ($\rho(r) \propto 1/r^2$) at larger $r$\footnote{This relation does not hold for $r\rightarrow\infty$ as $M(r)$ would diverge.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: We can introduce an effective cut-off radius $r_c$ from which $M(r\geq r_c)$ is constant.. .
elements.sent-2411-127: DM designates this exotic type of matter.
elements.sent-2411-128: The left panel of Figure~\ref{fig:rotcurve illustrates the point, showing the rotation curve of the galaxy NGC 6503 as an example~\cite{Begeman:1991iy , with the contribution of visible matter and DM, whereas the right panel shows the same behavior in a sample of spiral galaxies~\cite{1999ApJ...523..136S .}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}[c]{0.38\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{rotation.png} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}[c]{0.6\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{allrotation.png} \end{subfigure} \caption{Left panel: Rotation curve of the galaxy NGC 6503~\cite{Begeman:1991iy}. The dashed line represents the contribution from visible components, the dotted one from the gas, the dot-dashed one from the DM halo and the solid one the sum. Right panel: Rotation curves of a sample of numerous spiral galaxies~\cite{1999ApJ...523..136S}.} \label{fig:rotcurve}
elements.sent-2411-129: Another explanation to this discrepancy was proposed by Mordehai Milgrom in 1983, and involves a modification of Newton's second law in the regime of low accelerations~\cite{Milgrom:1983ca : $F = m \,a\, \mu(a/a_0)$ where $a_0$ defines the interface between the high and low-acceleration regimes, and $\mu(x)$ is an interpolation function that satisfies $\mu(x\gg1) = 1$ (to retrieve Newtonian dynamics) and $\mu(x\ll1) = x$.}
elements.sent-2411-130: When $a\ll a_0$ we have $F = m a^2/a_0 = m (v_c^2/r)^2/a_0$, and when applied to the same system as above we obtain \begin{equation}\frac{m}{a_0} \left(\frac{v_c^2(r)}{r}\right)^2 = m \frac{GM(r)}{r^2} \implies v_c(r) = (a_0 GM(r))^{1/4}\;,\end{equation} and therefore $v_c$ is a constant for $r$ larger than the spatial extension of all visible matter in the galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-131: This is the founding principle of Modified Newtonian Dynamics (or MOND), where the main goal is to explain the discrepancy in rotation curves of spiral galaxies without introducing any exotic matter.
elements.sent-2411-132: While this solution is arguably elegant, it does not constitute a realistic candidate to explain the discrepancy at the scale of galaxy clusters or some properties of the CMB anisotropies.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Evidence at the scale of galaxy clusters
elements.sent-2411-133: Actually, DM was first postulated at the scale of galaxy clusters.
elements.sent-2411-134: In 1933, Fritz Zwicky computed the mass of the Coma Cluster~\cite{Zwicky:1933gu by measuring the velocity dispersion of galaxies inside it.}
elements.sent-2411-135: This can be done by using the virial theorem, assuming the Coma cluster to be a stationary system.
elements.sent-2411-136: This theorem links the average kinetic energy $\langle T\rangle$ and potential energy $\langle U\rangle$ of the system through the relation $2\langle T\rangle + \langle U\rangle = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-137: On one hand, the kinetic energy is simply written as $\langle T\rangle = M\langle v^2 \rangle /2$ where $M$ is the mass of the cluster and $v$ the velocity of individual galaxies in the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-138: On the other hand, when assuming the distribution of galaxies in the cluster to be uniform in a sphere of radius $R$, we obtain $\langle U\rangle = -3GM^2/(5R)$.
elements.sent-2411-139: Therefore the velocity dispersion is \begin{equation}\label{eq:veldisp} \sigma_v = \sqrt{\langle v^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{\frac{3GM}{5R}} \sim 80\,km/s\;,\end{equation} since the Coma cluster has a radius of $R \simeq 0.3$ pc and contains around 800 galaxies with an individual mass of $10^9 M_\odot$.
elements.sent-2411-140: However the measured value of $\sigma_v$ lies around $1000$ km/s.
elements.sent-2411-141: Paired with the measurement of the mass-to-light ratio of $M/L\sim 500M_\odot/L_\odot$, it again suggests the presence of a dominant component of invisible matter in the Coma cluster.
elements.sent-2411-142: As the time goes by, other techniques have been developed to measure the mass of a galaxy cluster and are complementary to the one using kinematics and the virial theorem.
elements.sent-2411-143: The first one is by measuring the $X$-ray surface brightness of the target cluster.
elements.sent-2411-144: Galaxy clusters contain hot gas with temperature ranging from around $10$ to $100$ keV.
elements.sent-2411-145: The ionised electrons can produce $X$-rays through two processes: i) inverse Compton scattering (ICS) on CMB photons and ii) bremsstrahlung with ions in the gas.
elements.sent-2411-146: Measurements of the $X$-ray spectrum and intensity can infer the temperature $T_gas$ of the hot intracluster gas and its density $n_gas$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-147: Then, to relate these measurements with the cluster mass $M$, we have to combine the hydrostatic equilibrium equation of the gas (assuming spherical symmetry) \begin{equation}\label{eq:hydroeq} \frac{\partial P_gas}{\partial r}=-\mu_gas n_gas\frac{\partial\Phi}{\partial r}\;,\end{equation} with the Poisson equation of the cluster's content \begin{equation}\label{eq:poisson} \vec{\nabla}^2\Phi = 4\pi G \rho_tot\implies \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial r} = \frac{4\pi G}{r^2}\int_0^r dr'r'^2\rho_tot(r') = \frac{GM}{r^2}\;,\end{equation} where $P_gas$ is the gas pressure, $\mu_gas$ its mean atomic (or molecular) mass and $M$ the total mass of the cluster within a radius $r$.
elements.sent-2411-148: Combining Equations~\ref{eq:hydroeq and \ref{eq:poisson as well as adding the ideal gas law $P_gas = n_gas k_B T_gas$, we get \begin{equation}k_B\frac{\partial \left(n_gasT_gas\right)}{\partial r} = -\mu_gasn_gas\frac{GM}{r^2}\;,\end{equation} and after some work we can write \begin{equation}M = - \frac{k_Br^2}{G\mu_gasn_gas}\frac{\partial \left(n_gasT_gas\right)}{\partial r} = - \frac{k_BT_gasr}{G\mu_gas}\left[\frac{r}{n_gas}\frac{\partial n_gas}{\partial r} + \frac{r}{T_gas}\frac{\partial T_gas}{\partial r}\right] = -\frac{k_BT_gasr}{G\mu_gas}\left[\frac{\partial\ln n_gas}{\partial\ln r}+\frac{\partial\ln T_gas}{\partial\ln r}\right]\;.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}M = - \frac{k_Br^2}{G\mu_gasn_gas}\frac{\partial \left(n_gasT_gas\right)}{\partial r} = - \frac{k_BT_gasr}{G\mu_gas}\left[\frac{r}{n_gas}\frac{\partial n_gas}{\partial r} + \frac{r}{T_gas}\frac{\partial T_gas}{\partial r}\right] = -\frac{k_BT_gasr}{G\mu_gas}\left[\frac{\partial\ln n_gas}{\partial\ln r}+\frac{\partial\ln T_gas}{\partial\ln r}\right]\;.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-149: Thanks to this equation we are able to link properties of the $X$-ray spectrum from a galaxy cluster to its total mass.
elements.sent-2411-150: First uses of this observational technique in 1971 limited the amount of hot intracluster gas to less than 2\% of what is required for gravitational binding~\cite{1971Natur.231..107M .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Another technique to infer the mass of a galaxy cluster is through weak gravitational lensing.
elements.sent-2411-152: Here the cluster acts as a `gravitational lens' which deflects the light coming from a source that is behind it from the perspective of the observer, resulting in perceived slight shape distortions of the source.
elements.sent-2411-153: An example of this effect is shown in the right panel of Figure~\ref{fig:grav-lens , where the galaxy cluster MACS J1206 acts as the gravitational lens to some other background galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Here we use the thin lens approximation, meaning that we assume the distance between the source, lens and observer to be much larger than the size of the lens (i.e.\ the source and lens are considered to be point-like).
elements.sent-2411-155: The left panel of Figure~\ref{fig:grav-lens illustrates a gravitational lens system and defines all of the relevant angles and lengths we are about to use.}

elements.sent-2411-157: Also, from Figure~\ref{fig:grav-lens we can notice that the distance from the source $D_s$ and between the lens and the source $D_{ds}$ can be linked through \begin{equation}\label{eq:geometry} \theta D_s = \beta D_s + \hat{\alpha}(\xi)D_{ds}\;,\end{equation} and finally by inserting Equation~\ref{eq:deflect in Equation~\ref{eq:geometry and realising that $\xi = \theta D_d$ from Figure~\ref{fig:grav-lens , we get \begin{equation}\label{eq:lenseq} \beta(\theta) = \theta - \frac{D_{ds}}{D_d D_s}\frac{4GM}{c^2\theta}\;.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: To ease up the notation, we introduce the Einstein radius $\theta_E$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:redlenseq} \theta_E = \sqrt{\frac{4GM}{c^2}\frac{D_{ds}}{D_d D_s}} \implies \beta(\theta) = \theta - \frac{\theta_E^2}{\theta}\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-159: In the context of strong gravitational lensing, when the lens and source are perfectly aligned with the observer ($\beta = 0$), the image would be a ring of angular radius $\theta_E$, namely an Einstein ring.
elements.sent-2411-160: Solving Equation~\ref{eq:redlenseq provide us with two possible images \begin{equation}\label{eq:lenseqsol} \theta_\pm = \frac{1}{2}\left(\beta \pm \sqrt{\beta^2+4\theta_E^2}\right)\;.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-161: The surface brightness of the source and the image should be the same as dictated by Liouville's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-162: Therefore the total flux received by the image should vary by a factor $\mu$ named magnification which is the ratio between the image and source solid angles.

elements.sent-2411-165: When $\theta_\pm < \theta_E$, we get $\mu_\pm < 0$, meaning that the two images switched their positions from the observer's perspective.
elements.sent-2411-166: Therefore the total magnification should be \begin{equation}\mu = \lvert\mu_+\rvert + \lvert\mu_-\rvert = \frac{u^2+2}{u\sqrt{u^2+4}}\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-167: Even though we considered a very simplified model, it still shows that weak gravitational lensing observables can help us in inferring the mass of a galaxy cluster.

elements.sent-2411-168: A very strong evidence of the existence of DM in the Universe, that also shows how important it is to have complementarity in observational techniques, is the Bullet Cluster.
elements.sent-2411-169: This is a system of two colliding galaxy clusters $3.72$ billion light-years away from Earth that has been observed by the {\sc Magellan space probe and the {\sc Hubble Space Telescope in the optical range, by the {\sc Chandra $X$-ray Observatory and where weak gravitational lensing measurements have been performed (see Figure~\ref{fig:bullet ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: While optical observations show that individual galaxies were not much affected by the collision, the $X$-ray map shows that the two intracluster gas clouds have interacted electromagnetically with each other during the collision, therefore heated up and slowed down.
elements.sent-2411-171: But most importantly, weak gravitational lensing measurements show that the mass distribution of the merging cluster is not centred on the interacting gas clouds.
elements.sent-2411-172: Instead, it is observed that two massive halos have passed through each other during the collision.
elements.sent-2411-173: While the halos are collisionless almost in the same fashion as the individual galaxies, the mass of the halos is measured to be way greater than the total mass of the galaxies, meaning that the halos are mostly constituted of DM.
elements.sent-2411-174: Observations of dozens of other merging galaxy clusters~\cite{Harvey:2015hha also back up this inference.}
elements.sent-2411-175: This also constitutes a problem for the MOND interpretation of the missing mass problem, since MOND assumes that all matter is baryonic and therefore we would see the mass distribution of a merging cluster to be centred around the interacting intracluster gas clouds.
elements.sent-2411-176: In the previous subsections, we showed that DM is a compelling solution to the missing mass problem at the scale of galaxies and galaxy clusters.
elements.sent-2411-177: However, these observations are not able to infer the total amount of DM in the whole Universe.
elements.sent-2411-178: For this, we turn to cosmological probes of DM, especially measurements of the temperatures anisotropies in the CMB.
elements.sent-2411-179: The CMB originated during the recombination epoch (378,000 years after the Big Bang).
elements.sent-2411-180: Before this epoch, the Universe was very hot and contained unbound electrons and protons, therefore it was opaque to radiation.
elements.sent-2411-181: As the Universe continued to expand, its temperature decreased, thus free electrons and protons could form the first hydrogen atoms.
elements.sent-2411-182: Radiation was then able to propagate through this new state of matter.
elements.sent-2411-183: The CMB is the oldest source of radiation we will ever be able to probe.
elements.sent-2411-184: It was actually discovered inadvertently by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson in 1965~\cite{1965ApJ...142..419P .}
elements.sent-2411-185: The properties of the CMB are essentially the ones of a black body of temperature $T_0 = 2.7255 \pm 0.0006$ K~\cite{Fixsen:2009ug while showing some temperature anisotropies at the $10^{-5}$ level across a wide range of angular scales.}
elements.sent-2411-186: The left panel of Figure~\ref{fig:CMB shows a map of these anisotropies measured by {\sc Planck ~\cite{Planck:2018nkj .}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: To take them into account, the CMB temperature at a specific position in the sky $(\theta, \phi)$ can be written in the following expansion \begin{equation}T(\theta, \phi) = T_0\;\sum_{l=0}^{+\infty}\sum_{m=-\ell}^{+\ell} a_{\ell m} Y_{\ell m}(\theta,\phi)\;,\end{equation} where $Y_{\ell m (\theta,\phi)$ are the spherical harmonics.}

elements.sent-2411-191: Before further developing this topic it would be useful to do a little reminder on cosmology and the $\Lambda$CDM model.
elements.sent-2411-192: First measurements of radial velocities of galaxies by Vesto Slipher in 1917 have suggested that most of the galaxies are moving away from us~\cite{1917PAPhS..56..403S .}
elements.sent-2411-193: This statement was then backed up by Edwin Hubble's work in 1929~\cite{1929PNAS...15..168H .}
elements.sent-2411-194: To explain this phenomenon, Alexander Friedman and Georges Lema\^itre proposed respectively in 1922 and 1927 that the Universe is expanding~\cite{Friedman:1922kd,Lemaitre:1927zz .}
elements.sent-2411-195: The radial velocity $v$ is inferred using the Fizeau-Doppler effect: galaxies moving away from Earth emit radiation at a wavelength $\lambda_e$ that will undergo a redshift and arrive at Earth with a greater wavelength $\lambda_o$.
elements.sent-2411-197: We observe that galaxies moving away from us follow the Hubble-Lema\^itre law, where their radial velocity is proportional to their distance from the Earth: $v = H_0\,d$ where $H_0$ is known as the Hubble constant.
elements.sent-2411-198: The founding ansatz of cosmology is the cosmological principle: the Universe is homogeneous and isotropic at very large scales.
elements.sent-2411-199: The metric of an expanding Universe that follows this principle is called the Friedmann-Lema\^itre-Robertson-Walker (FLRW) metric, where the associated infinitesimal spacetime interval is written (in spherical coordinates $x^\mu = (t, r, \theta, \phi)$) \begin{equation}ds^2 = dt^2 - a^2(t) \left[\frac{dr^2}{1-kr^2}+r^2\left(d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta d\phi^2\right)\right]\;,\end{equation} where the $(+,-,-,-)$ metric signature is adopted.
elements.sent-2411-200: The scale factor $a(t)$ encodes the expansion of the Universe, whereas the curvature of the Universe is represented by $k$.
elements.sent-2411-201: The three special values of $k$ are: i) $k=1$ for a closed universe with a spherical geometry, ii) $k=0$ for a flat one, obeying Euclidean geometry and iii) $k=-1$ for an open one with an hyperbolic geometry.
elements.sent-2411-202: The redshift $z$ can now be expressed in term of the scale factor \begin{equation}\frac{\lambda_o}{a_0} = \frac{\lambda_e}{a(t)} \implies z = \frac{a_0}{a(t)} - 1\;,\end{equation} where $x_0$ denotes the value of a physical quantity $x$ today.
elements.sent-2411-203: Now we have to link the expansion of the Universe with its contents.

elements.sent-2411-209: If a positive cosmological constant dominates the Universe today, this can explain its expansion, since its pressure would be negative.
elements.sent-2411-210: Now by injecting the FLRW metric in the left-hand side of Equation~\ref{eq:EFE and the stress-energy tensor in the right-hand-side, we obtain the two Friedmann equations \begin{equation}\label{eq:friedmann} \frac{\dot{a}^2+k}{a^2} = \frac{8\pi G}{3}\sum_{i\in\{fluids + \Lambda\}} \rho_i \quad and \quad \frac{\ddot{a}}{a}=-\frac{4\pi G}{3}\sum_{i\in\{fluids + \Lambda\}} \rho_i\left(1+3w_i\right)\;,\end{equation} where the sum over the fluids $i$ includes now the cosmological constant.}
elements.sent-2411-211: If we consider only one kind of fluid, differentiating the first equation and injecting it into the second one gives a conservation equation \begin{equation}\frac{\dot{\rho}}{\rho} = -3(1+w)\frac{\dot{a}}{a}\;,\end{equation} that allows us to write $\rho$ in terms of the redshift $z$ \begin{equation}\rho = \rho_0 \left(\frac{a}{a_0}\right)^{-3(1+w)} = \rho_0 (1+z)^{3(1+w)}\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-212: By introducing the Hubble parameter $H = \dot{a /a$ and the cosmological parameters \begin{equation}\label{eq:cosmoparam} \Omega_{i,0} = \rho_{i,0} / \rho_{c,0} \quad and \quad \Omega_{k,0} = -k/(a_0^2H_0^2)\;,\end{equation} where $\rho_{c,0 = 3H_0^2/(8\pi G)$, we can rewrite the first Friedmann equation \begin{equation}\label{eq:reducfriedmann} \left(\frac{H}{H_0}\right)^2 = \sum_{i\in\{fluids + \Lambda + k\}} \Omega_{i,0}(1+z)^{3(1+w_i)}\;,\end{equation} where $w_k = -1/3$ is an effective definition since the curvature does not obey an equation of state.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: We can see from this equation that the sum of the cosmological parameters today ($z=0$) is normalized to 1.
elements.sent-2411-214: Now let us consider the flat $\Lambda$CDM model.
elements.sent-2411-215: In this model, the Universe has no curvature ($k=0$) and contains baryonic matter and DM ($w_b = w_DM = 0$), radiation ($w_r = 1/3$) and the cosmological constant ($w_\Lambda = -1$).
elements.sent-2411-216: Therefore Equation~\ref{eq:reducfriedmann is written in this case \begin{equation}H = H_0 \sqrt{\Omega_{m,0} (1+z)^3 + \Omega_{r,0} (1+z)^4 + \Omega_{\Lambda,0}}\;,\end{equation} where $\Omega_{m,0 = \Omega_{b,0 + \Omega_{DM,0}$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-217: By fitting the flat $\Lambda$CDM model to the CMB temperature anisotropies measured by {\sc Planck , we obtain the following abundances for baryonic matter and DM today~\cite{Planck:2018vyg \begin{equation}\Omega_{b,0} = 0.0493 \pm 0.0006 \quad and \quad \Omega_{DM,0} = 0.265 \pm 0.007\;,\end{equation} which shows that the Universe contains $5.375$ times more DM than baryonic matter.}}
elements.sent-2411-218: Also, {\sc Planck measures a positive cosmological constant that dominates the Universe today, and a negligible radiation abundance: \begin{equation}\Omega_{\Lambda,0} = 0.685 \pm 0.007 \quad and \quad \Omega_{r,0} = (5.38 \pm 0.15) \times 10^{-5}\;,\end{equation} The evolution of the cosmological parameters in the $\Lambda$CDM model is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:cosmoparam .}}
elements.sent-2411-219: In this model, the Universe goes first through a radiation dominated era until $z \sim 3400$ to then a matter dominated era until $z \sim 0.3$.
elements.sent-2411-220: We now live in an Universe dominated by the cosmological constant and therefore in accelerating expansion, according to the second Friedmann equation ($\ddot{a /a = \Lambda/3 >0$).}
elements.sent-2411-221: On a side note, the $\Lambda$CDM model provides an excellent fit to the measured CMB temperature anisotropies and allows us to infer a precise value for the Hubble constant: $H_0 = 67.4\pm 0.5$~km/s/Mpc~\cite{Planck:2018vyg .}
elements.sent-2411-222: However, this value is in tension with what we get from the Hubble-Lema\^itre law using cosmic distance ladder measurements, where $H_0 = 73\pm1$ km/s/Mpc~\cite{Riess:2021jrx .}
elements.sent-2411-223: This is known as the Hubble tension and remains to this day one of the greatest problems of modern cosmology.
elements.sent-2411-224: From now on, we choose to use the {\sc Planck /$\Lambda$CDM value of $H_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-225: So far, we have mentioned some of the evidences for the existence of DM, but we have yet to ask ourselves about its nature.
elements.sent-2411-226: In the following section, we describe the possible production mechanisms of DM as a particle, then mention what makes a good DM candidate to finally list some of the best known ones.
elements.sent-2411-227: Although we are aware that the existence of DM is justified, its nature remains elusive.
elements.sent-2411-228: This section does not have the ambition to summarise every possible production mechanism for DM nor enumerate every DM candidates.
elements.sent-2411-229: We limit ourselves to some of the standard scenarios of particle DM production in the early Universe~\cite{Mambrini:2021 , while listing some of the best known candidates.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Before recombination, the Universe was filled with diverse species of particles.
elements.sent-2411-231: Each species is characterised by their phase-space distribution $f(x^\mu, p^\mu)$ and may interact with other species to achieve thermal equilibrium with the primordial plasma of temperature $T$.
elements.sent-2411-232: The interaction rate per particle $\Gamma_i$ associated to a species $i$ is related to the average cross section of interaction times the relative velocity $\langle\sigma v\rangle_{ij}$ between $i$ and other species $j$ and the number density of targets per volume $n_j$ \begin{equation}\Gamma_i = \sum_j n_j \langle\sigma v\rangle_{ij}\;,\end{equation} where $j$ can also be $i$ to take into account self-interactions.
elements.sent-2411-233: As the Universe expands, the interaction rate $\Gamma_i$ decreases since $n_j \propto 1/V \sim a^{-3}$, where $V$ is the volume of the Universe.
elements.sent-2411-234: When $\Gamma_i \lesssim H$, the species $i$ is decoupled from the primordial plasma, meaning that it no longer interacts with other species nor with itself, therefore its comoving density $n_i a^3$ remains constant\footnote{One can see that $\Gamma_i \lesssim H$ implies $\Gamma_i^{-1 \gtrsim H^{-1}$, where $\Gamma_i^{-1}$ is the mean free time of particles and $H^{-1}$ is approximately the age of the Universe at the time.}}
elements.sent-2411-235: This condition $\Gamma_i \lesssim H$ therefore imposes that particles are very unlikely to interact with one to another.. .
elements.sent-2411-236: Moreover it means that this species is no longer in thermal equilibrium with the plasma, hence has its own temperature $T_i$ different from the plasma one.
elements.sent-2411-237: Recalling the cosmological principle, the phase-space distribution of the species of particles depends only on the magnitude of the momentum $p=\lvert \vec{p \rvert$ of individual particles and time $x^0 = t$.}
elements.sent-2411-238: We might as well use the temperature of the species $T_i$ as the evolutionary parameter instead of $t$, where $T_i = T$ if the species is coupled to the plasma or $T_i \neq T$ in the opposite case.
elements.sent-2411-239: We then can write some useful thermodynamical quantities associated to the different species, such as their number density per unit volume $n_i$, energy density per unit volume $\rho_i$, pressure $P_i$ and entropy density per unit volume $s_i$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:n} n_i(T_i) = g_i\int \frac{d^3\vec{p}}{(2\pi)^3} f(p,T_i)\;, \label{eq:rho} \rho_i(T_i) = g_i\int \frac{d^3\vec{p}}{(2\pi)^3} E(p)f(p,T_i)\;, \label{eq:P} P_i(T_i) = g_i\int \frac{d^3\vec{p}}{(2\pi)^3} \frac{p^2}{3E(p)}f(p,T_i)\;, \label{eq:s} s_i(T_i) = \frac{\rho(T_i) + P(T_i)}{T_i}\;,\end{equation} where $E(p) = \sqrt{p^2+m_i^2}$ and $g_i$ is the number of internal degrees of freedom of the particles constituting the gas, for example the number of spin, helicity or colour states.
elements.formula-2411-032: \begin{equation}\label{eq:n} n_i(T_i) = g_i\int \frac{d^3\vec{p}}{(2\pi)^3} f(p,T_i)\;, \label{eq:rho} \rho_i(T_i) = g_i\int \frac{d^3\vec{p}}{(2\pi)^3} E(p)f(p,T_i)\;, \label{eq:P} P_i(T_i) = g_i\int \frac{d^3\vec{p}}{(2\pi)^3} \frac{p^2}{3E(p)}f(p,T_i)\;, \label{eq:s} s_i(T_i) = \frac{\rho(T_i) + P(T_i)}{T_i}\;,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-240: The smallest phase-space volume a particle of the gas can occupy is $h^3$ according to quantum mechanics, hence the presence of the factor $(2\pi)^3$ in natural units.
elements.sent-2411-241: Thermal equilibrium in the primordial plasma is realised through to the following process \begin{equation}e^\pm + \gamma \longrightarrow e^\pm + \gamma\;,\end{equation} while the chemical equilibrium is achieved by the following processes \begin{equation}e^+ + e^- \longleftrightarrow 2\gamma\;, e^+ + e^- \longleftrightarrow 3\gamma\;.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-299: Although we still do not know about the nature of DM, some of its properties are well constrained:.
elements.sent-2411-300: Electric charge: DM remains invisible even with the sensitivity of our current observational methods.
elements.sent-2411-301: Although DM is often assumed to be electrically neutral, some theoretical models allow DM to have a non-zero electric charge (known as millicharge DM).
elements.sent-2411-302: The most stringent lower limits on the DM charge are $3.2\times 10^{-11 (m_DM/10\,GeV)^{0.52}$ for $m_DM \gtrsim 10$ GeV and $1.0\times 10^{-9 (m_DM/10\,GeV)^{0.25}$ for $m_DM \lesssim 10$ GeV, in units of the electron charge~\cite{Dunsky:2018mqs,cirelli2024dark .}}}
elements.sent-2411-303: Self-interactions: Observations of colliding galaxy clusters show that DM should have limited self-interactions (recalling what we stated in Section~\ref{subsec:galclust ).}
elements.sent-2411-304: Constraints on the DM self-interaction cross section yield $\sigma_DM-DM/m_DM < 8.4 \times 10^{-25}$ cm$^2$/GeV~\cite{Harvey:2015hha .}
elements.sent-2411-305: Lifetime: The presence of DM in our Universe today indicates that DM has to be stable over cosmological timescales.
elements.sent-2411-306: Its decay time should therefore be large compared to the age of the Universe.
elements.sent-2411-307: Current constraints on the DM lifetime lead to $\tau_DM \gtrsim 10^{28}$ s for $m_DM \simeq 1$ GeV~\cite{DelaTorreLuque:2023olp .}
elements.sent-2411-308: There exist some limits on the mass of DM, although the current allowed mass range for DM currently spans up to 90 orders of magnitude.

elements.sent-2411-337: The fact that $\bar{\Theta}$ is so small without any apparent reason is known as the strong $CP$ problem.
elements.sent-2411-338: A solution to this problem was proposed by Roberto Peccei and Helen Quinn in 1977~\cite{Peccei:1977ur, Peccei:1977hh , which is to add a new global symmetry to the SM that can be spontaneously broken, effectively promoting $\bar{\Theta}$ to a field whose minimum is $0$.}
elements.sent-2411-339: Frank Wilczek and Steven Weinberg then independently showed that such a symmetry breaking would give rise to a pseudo-Nambu-Goldstone boson: the axion~\cite{Wilczek:1977pj, Weinberg:1977ma .}

elements.sent-2411-341: The properties of the axion fit the ones mentioned in Section~\ref{subsec:DMprop , making it a suitable DM candidate.}
elements.sent-2411-342: The parameter space for the QCD axion is quite restricted, as it can be shown that all of the allowed couplings between the axion and SM particles are essentially a power-law of the axion mass.
elements.sent-2411-343: To open up the parameter space, other BSM models can predict particles with the same properties as the axion, while alleviating the dependency of the couplings to the particle mass.
elements.sent-2411-344: Such particles are called axion-like particles.

elements.sent-2411-347: However, experiments that probe neutrinos from the Sun, Earth's atmosphere, nuclear reactors and particle accelerators have all shown that neutrinos can undergo flavor oscillations, a phenomenon that occur only if the neutrinos are massive.
elements.sent-2411-348: The current lower bound on the sum of neutrino masses is $\sum m_\nu \gtrsim 0.06$ eV~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk .}
elements.sent-2411-349: Moreover, the SM does not predict the existence of right-handed neutrinos (nor left-handed antineutrinos), whereas the other SM fermions exist in both helicities.

elements.sent-2411-352: Primordial black holes: Instead of being a particle or a field, there is also a possibility that DM could be in the form of compact objects, named primordial black holes (PBHs).
elements.sent-2411-353: Instead of being formed during the collapse of a massive star, these black holes (BHs) are most likely formed from the collapse of overdense matter regions in the early Universe, but other scenarios are possible.

elements.sent-2411-357: Up to this point we have described some well known DM candidates, however the whole landscape is large.
elements.sent-2411-358: In phenomenological studies of DM, it is usual to assume DM to be model-independent, i.e.\ a field, particle or macroscopic object without presuming its production nor a model that predicts its existence.
elements.sent-2411-359: This allows to ease the readability and comparison between different phenomenological studies.
elements.sent-2411-360: In the following section, we describe the main experimental ways to probe particle DM.
elements.sent-2411-361: Detection methods that consist of trying to figure out the nature of particle DM can be put in three categories:.
elements.sent-2411-362: Collider and accelerator searches: producing DM through SM particle collisions,.
elements.sent-2411-363: Direct detection (DD): probing nuclear recoils from DM particles scattering on atomic nuclei,.
elements.sent-2411-364: Indirect detection (ID): looking at cosmic ray (CR) signals associated to DM annihilation or decay, or emissions from macroscopic DM.
elements.sent-2411-365: DD and ID can also be used to probe axionic DM, in addition to specific methods such as light-shining-through-wall or microwave cavity experiments.
elements.sent-2411-366: Macroscopic DM searches solely rely on astrophysical observations and ID.
elements.sent-2411-367: In this section, we limit ourselves to describe collider, accelerator searches and DD, as ID will be deeply covered in Chapter~\ref{chap:ID .}
elements.sent-2411-368: Specific detection methods for axions are beyond the scope of this thesis.
elements.sent-2411-369: In order to truly put a finger on the nature of DM, we have to rely on the complementary of the aforementioned detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-370: A possible DM signature appearing in experiments using one method should be confronted to a matching signal in experiments using other methods.
elements.sent-2411-371: A possible way to probe DM is to actually produce it in colliders or in particle accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-372: At the Large Hadron Collider operated by the CERN, proton beams are accelerated in opposite directions and then collide, producing a complex mix of various particles that travel to detectors, such as {\sc Atlas or {\sc Cms , that have been set up around the collision points.}}
elements.sent-2411-373: The idea is that DM particles can be produced from these collisions, and not interact with the detectors, leading to an `observation' of missing energy/momentum in the transverse plane of the collisions.
elements.sent-2411-374: The same principle can be applied in other types of accelerators, where a particle beam can create DM by hitting a fixed target or a beam dump.
elements.sent-2411-375: These approaches are quite model-dependent, as we have to theorise what are the interactions between DM and SM particles that allow the latter to produce DM during collisions.
elements.sent-2411-376: DM production through an exchange of a $H$ or $Z$ boson are the simplest scenarios, but other theoretically motivated models can be considered e.g., supersymmetric models or hidden sectors.
elements.sent-2411-377: For now, no signal of missing energy has been observed and limits on the DM mass and couplings with the SM are set, depending on individual models.
elements.sent-2411-378: The interested reader can find details on on-going efforts and future initiatives in trying to probe DM in colliders and accelerators in~\cite{Battaglieri:2017aum .}
elements.sent-2411-379: In 1985, Mark Goodman and Edward Witten were the first to discuss the possibility of detecting DM particles inhabiting the Galactic halo by probing their scattering off of nuclei in a detector~\cite{Goodman:1984dc .}
elements.sent-2411-380: The recoil spectrum $dR/dE_r$, as a function of the nuclear recoil energy $E_r$, is written \begin{equation}\frac{dR}{dE_r}(E_r,t)=\frac{\rho_\odot M}{m_Nm_DM} \int_{v_min}^{v_esc} d^3\vec{v}\ v f(\vec{v}+\vec{v}_\oplus(t))\frac{d\sigma}{dE_r}(E_r,v)\;,\end{equation} where $M$ is the target mass of the detector, $m_N$ is the mass of the target nucleus, $\rho_\odot$ is the local DM energy density (which is around $0.4$ GeV/cm$^3$), $v = \lvert\vec{v}\rvert$ is the velocity of DM particles in the detector rest frame, $f(\vec{v +\vec{v _\oplus(t))$ their velocity distribution in Earth's rest frame, usually assumed to be roughly Maxwellian\footnote{$f(v) \propto e^{-v^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ where $\sigma \simeq 220 - 270$ km/s in the MW~\cite{Herzog-Arbeitman:2017fte .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-381: The minimal speed required for a DM particle to induce a nuclear recoil of energy $E_r$ is $v_min = \sqrt{E_rm_N/(2\mu^2)}$ with $\mu = m_Nm_DM/(m_N+m_DM)$ being the reduced mass of the DM-nucleus system, and the maximal velocity of a DM particle is its escape velocity from the gravitational well of the MW $v_esc$.
elements.sent-2411-382: Essentially the recoil spectrum depends on the properties of the target (DD experiments are using a large variety of targets, such as Ge, liquid Xe, liquid Ar, NaI, etc.), the DM mass $m_DM$ and the DM-nucleon spin-(in)dependent cross sections $\sigma_SD$ ($\sigma_SI$).
elements.sent-2411-383: Due to the motion of the Earth in the Galactic rest frame, which is a superposition of the Earth's orbit around the Sun and the Sun's motion around the Galactic centre (GC), in principle we should observe an annual modulation of the recoil spectrum in DD experiments.
elements.sent-2411-384: On June 2, Earth's velocity in the Galactic rest frame is maximal (the Earth faces a `DM wind' due to the motion of the Sun), and the recoil spectrum is also at its maximum.
elements.sent-2411-385: On the contrary, on December 2, the Earth faces a slightly weaker DM wind hence the recoil spectrum is at its minimum.
elements.sent-2411-386: Since the velocity of the Sun around the GC (around $250$ km/s) is way greater than the velocity of the Earth around the Sun (around $30$ km/s), the amplitude of expected annual modulation in the recoil spectrum should be around $5\%$.

elements.sent-2411-398: However, we conveniently avoided the subject of DM ID until now.
elements.sent-2411-399: The goal of the following chapter is to provide insight into what this type of detection entails, as it is the main topic of this thesis.
elements.sent-2411-400: The aim of ID is to look for the products of particle DM annihilation or decay, as well as those from PBH evaporation.
elements.sent-2411-401: Such products can be in the form of stable, charged CRs, photons, and neutrinos that propagate from their production point through the (extra-)galactic medium until they reach our detectors.
elements.sent-2411-402: Figure~\ref{fig:DM ID illustrates the principle of this detection method.}
elements.sent-2411-403: Each product carries different types of information, making the ability to detect multiple types of products highly beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-404: On one hand, photons and neutrinos are not deflected by surrounding magnetic fields (since they are neutral) and therefore allow us to precisely trace their source of emission.
elements.sent-2411-405: On the other hand, charged CRs can include antiparticles that can be detected with a low astrophysical background, since antiparticles are not easily produced by astrophysical processes.

elements.sent-2411-407: In this section we explore some of the most promising targets for DM ID.
elements.sent-2411-408: Essentially, the ideal target should meet two criteria: i) it has to be DM-rich to increase the likelihood of detecting a strong signal from it and ii) it has to be quite poor in astrophysical sources, as disentangling an indirect DM signal from the astrophysical background can be challenging.
elements.sent-2411-409: However, there is no perfect target and the ones we investigate in this section will always be subject to a compromise between the two criteria.
elements.sent-2411-410: We start by looking at the case of celestial bodies (Section~\ref{subsec:bodies ) and our own Galaxy (Section~\ref{subsec:MW ), to zoom out to the Local Group and consider the satellite dSphs of the MW (Section~\ref{subsec:dSph ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-411: Finally we investigate targets outside of the Local Group, such as galaxy clusters (Section~\ref{subsec:galclustID ).}
elements.sent-2411-412: There is a possibility that DM particles from the MW halo could be captured by celestial bodies, such as stars and planets.
elements.sent-2411-413: This process can happen when DM particles successively scatter with the nuclei of a celestial body, in turn loosing kinetic energy and eventually sinking into its centre.
elements.sent-2411-414: Over time, they accumulate and essentially create a DM over-density in the centre of the body, that increases the production of indirect signals.
elements.sent-2411-415: In particular, this idea has been explored since the end of the 1980s, especially in the case of DM capture in the Sun~\cite{Gould:1987ir,Gould:1987ju,Gould:1987ww .}
elements.sent-2411-416: In this scenario, the only DM-produced particles that could escape the Sun are the neutrinos, and therefore detecting an excess of neutrinos from the Sun on top of the solar neutrino background (mainly from nuclear fusion) can be identified as a signal from DM.
elements.sent-2411-417: Typically the DM-produced neutrinos can have energies in the GeV--TeV range, whereas around the MeV for neutrinos produced by the Sun.
elements.sent-2411-418: Another credible possibility consists of DM annihilating into mediators particles that escape the Sun, that can then decay into any SM particle~\cite{Batell:2009zp,Schuster:2009au .}
elements.sent-2411-419: The formalism for computing signals from DM captured in celestial objects is thoroughly described in~\cite{Leane:2023woh , accompanied by a numerical framework named \verb|Asteria|~\cite{rebecca_k_leane_2023_8150110 .}}
elements.sent-2411-420: We previously mentioned in Section~\ref{subsec:rotcurves that galaxies are embedded in a DM halo that extends beyond their disk, which explains the flatness of their rotation curve at high distances from their centre.}
elements.sent-2411-421: Our own Galaxy is therefore an excellent target for ID, since we are literally bathing in its DM halo.
elements.sent-2411-422: A crucial ingredient to compute indirect signals from DM in the MW is the distribution of its energy density $\rho_DM(\vec{x )$.}
elements.sent-2411-423: It is believed that galaxies form through the gravitational collapse of giant clouds of gas and DM.
elements.sent-2411-424: Baryonic matter can dissipate its energy through interactions with itself and in order to conserve its angular momentum during its cooling, the initially spherically symmetric gas cloud will rearrange itself into a disk.
elements.sent-2411-425: On the contrary, DM has limited interactions with itself and baryonic matter, thus cannot dissipate energy during the collapse: the distribution of DM remains approximately spherically symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-426: Therefore, when describing the DM distribution in a galaxy, it is usual to adopt a profile that is radial, i.e.\ only dependent on the Galactocentric distance $r$.
elements.sent-2411-427: The DM distribution in our Galaxy is still not known, as it is very challenging to reconstruct it while being inside the halo.
elements.sent-2411-428: Therefore, we have to assume a certain parametrisation of the profile when we are trying to predict indirect signals of DM in the MW.
elements.sent-2411-429: This would thus weaken the robustness of the prediction, especially when we are looking at a region of interest (ROI) close to the GC, as their behavior at $r\rightarrow 0$ can significantly differ from one profile to another, as we will see below.
elements.sent-2411-430: The most popular parametrisations of the DM profile are the following:.
elements.sent-2411-431: The Navarro-Frenk-White (NFW) profile~\cite{Navarro:1995iw , which came from fitting the DM distribution of halos arising in seminal DM-only $N$-body simulations, remains to this day the standard benchmark for the DM profile in the MW.}
elements.sent-2411-432: Its functional form is \begin{equation}\label{eq:NFW} \rho_NFW(r) = \rho_s \left(\frac{r_s}{r}\right) \left(1+\frac{r}{r_s}\right)^{-2}\;,\end{equation} showing that the DM density behaves like $r^{-1}$ near the GC and therefore diverges when $r\rightarrow 0$.
elements.sent-2411-433: To control how steep the DM density increases near the GC, the NFW can be generalised by introducing a free parameter $\gamma$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:gNFW} \rho_gNFW(r) = \rho_s \left(\frac{r_s}{r}\right)^\gamma \left(1+\frac{r}{r_s}\right)^{\gamma-3}\;,\end{equation} where the standard NFW profile is retrieved when $\gamma=1$.
elements.sent-2411-434: In this chapter and the following ones, the case $\gamma=1.26$ will be referred to as the contracted NFW profile (cNFW), which provides a better fit to the $\gamma$-ray excess in the GC~\cite{Daylan:2014rsa .}
elements.sent-2411-435: We further develop on this topic later in this section.
elements.sent-2411-436: The Einasto profile~\cite{1965TrAlm...5...87E,Graham:2005xx provides a better fit to higher resolution $N$-body simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-437: Contrary to the NFW profile, this profile does not diverge when $r\rightarrow 0$.
elements.sent-2411-438: Its functional form is written \begin{equation}\rho_Ein(r) = \rho_s \exp\left\{-\frac{2}{\alpha_Ein}\left[\left(\frac{r}{r_s}\right)^{\alpha_Ein}-1\right]\right\}\;,\end{equation} where $\alpha_Ein = 0.17$ is an averaged value from fits to different $N$-body simulations.
elements.sent-2411-439: The Isothermal profile~\cite{1980ApJS...44...73B is obtained analytically by assuming DM particles are collisionless and therefore follow a Boltzmann velocity distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-440: When doing the computation, one gets $\rho(r) \propto r^{-2}$ which is divergent for $r\rightarrow 0$.
elements.sent-2411-441: To alleviate this issue, a cutoff radius $r_s$ can be introduced, for which $\rho(r\lesssim r_s)$ is constant.
elements.sent-2411-442: The expression of this profile is therefore \begin{equation}\label{eq:Isothermal} \rho_Iso(r) = \frac{\rho_s}{1+(r/r_s)^2}\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-443: This profile was actually very popular before $N$-body simulations were available due to its theoretical motivation.
elements.sent-2411-444: The Burkert profile~\cite{Burkert:1995yz initially arose from the measurement of rotation curves in a survey of dwarf galaxies, but then has proven to be a good fit to the ones of regular galaxies as well.}

elements.sent-2411-461: Now that we know more or less how DM is distributed in our Galaxy, we can start to think of which targets we should point our detectors to in order to probe indirect signals from DM.
elements.sent-2411-462: The most sensible answer would be to point them straight at the GC, since this is where the DM density is the highest.
elements.sent-2411-463: Actually, in recent years an excess of $\gamma$-rays has been reported in a region extending to a dozen of degrees around the GC~\cite{Hooper:2010mq that could be attributed to DM.}
elements.sent-2411-464: A more detailed analysis~\cite{Daylan:2014rsa claimed that this excess is best fit by DM particles with $m_DM\simeq 45$ GeV annihilating into $b\bar{b}$ with a cross section of $\langle\sigma v\rangle \simeq 3 \times 10^{-26}$ cm$^3$/s, and distributed along a cNFW profile (with $\gamma = 1.26$).}
elements.sent-2411-465: However, the GC is also extremely dense in astrophysical sources that our current detectors are not able to resolve.

elements.sent-2411-467: The other most credible explanation of the excess involves a high population of unresolved astrophysical sources, such as millisecond pulsars~\cite{Bartels:2015aea,Lee:2015fea .}
elements.sent-2411-468: When exploring higher Galactic latitudes, both of the astrophysical background and DM density are reduced.

elements.sent-2411-471: Our Galaxy is surrounded by a number of satellite dSphs that are promising targets for DM ID.
elements.sent-2411-472: First, the number of stars contained in dSphs can range between a few dozens to several thousands of stars and, in addition to that, they are scarce in interstellar gas.
elements.sent-2411-473: These observations imply that the astrophysical background coming from them is low.
elements.sent-2411-474: Second, measurements of their rotation curve and mass-to-light ratio indicate they are dominated by DM.
elements.sent-2411-475: Moreover they are positioned in relatively high Galactic latitudes, therefore their faint emission is not covered by any foreground emission from the Galactic plane (GP).
elements.sent-2411-476: In Table~\ref{tab:dSphs we list some of the measured properties of a few dSphs around our Galaxy.}
elements.sent-2411-477: Classical dSphs refer to the ones that were discovered before 1990, and therefore are the most luminous ones.
elements.sent-2411-478: The other category, ultrafaint dSphs, is straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-479: The latter can have a way higher mass-to-light ratio compared to the classical ones, e.g.\ the Bo\"otes II dSph reaches several thousands of $M_\odot/L_\odot$.
elements.sent-2411-480: The star scarcity of dSphs can be a double edge-sword.
elements.sent-2411-481: On one hand it implies that astrophysical emissions from a dSph are limited, therefore increasing the likelihood of detecting an indirect signal from DM.
elements.sent-2411-482: On the other hand, stars are the only visible tracers of the gravitational potential in dSphs, and their scarcity makes the reconstruction of the DM profile challenging.

elements.section-2411-005: Predicting indirect signals from dark matter in the Milky~Way
elements.sent-2411-488: The goal of this section is to provide an overview on the theoretical framework useful to predict indirect signals coming from DM in the Galactic halo.
elements.sent-2411-489: We choose to solely focus on this target, since the research conducted during this thesis (summarised in Chapters~\ref{chap:subGeV , \ref{chap:prop and \ref{chap:PBH ) has only involved DM searches in the MW.}}}
elements.sent-2411-490: We begin with the production of stable particles (Section~\ref{subsec:particleprod ), then investigate the propagation of DM-produced charged CRs (Section~\ref{subsec:CCRs ), photons and neutrinos (Section~\ref{subsec:gammaneutrinos ) in the interstellar medium (ISM) until possibly reaching our detectors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-491: In principle, DM particles can annihilate or decay into any particle-antiparticle pair, either from the SM or BSM, called final states, as long as it is kinematically possible\footnote{In the case where DM is non-relativistic, we can assume that DM particles annihilate (decay) at rest.}
elements.sent-2411-492: To insure the conservation of energy, $m_DM$ must be greater or equal than (half of) the mass of the final state particle.. .
elements.sent-2411-493: After that, the final states can undergo decays, hadronisation, coalescence and emit soft radiations.
elements.sent-2411-494: In the end, only stable particles such as $e^\pm$, $\overset{\scriptscriptstyle(-) {p}$, $\overset{\scriptscriptstyle(-) {d}$, $\gamma$ and $\overset{\scriptscriptstyle(-) {\nu _{e,\mu,\tau}$ survive and propagate through the ISM until possibly reaching our detectors.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-495: We usually consider that the annihilation or decay of DM particles and the production of stable ones are occurring at the same point, since the length scale at which the latter propagate is way larger than the one at which particle physics processes happen.
elements.sent-2411-496: The general expression of the energy spectrum at production $dN_i/dK_i$ (where $K$ is the kinetic energy) of stable particles $i$ is the sum of individual energy spectra at production over all annihilation or decay channels, weighted by their branching ratio \begin{equation}\label{eq:sumBR} \frac{dN_i}{dK_i} = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \displaystyle \sum_FS \frac{\langle\sigma v\rangle_FS}{\langle\sigma v\rangle_tot} \frac{dN_i^FS}{dK_i} = \sum_FS BR_FS \frac{dN_i^FS}{dK_i} (annihilation) \displaystyle \sum_FS \frac{\Gamma_FS}{\Gamma_tot} \frac{dN_i^FS}{dK_i} = \sum_FS BR_FS \frac{dN_i^FS}{dK_i} (decay) \end{array} \right.\;,\end{equation} where the branching ratios BR$_FS$ are expressed in terms of the annihilation cross section $\langle\sigma v\rangle_FS$ or decay rate $\Gamma_FS$ of DM into the final states FS and their sum over all final states $\langle\sigma v\rangle_tot$ and $\Gamma_tot$.}
elements.sent-2411-497: For a given BSM model, one can write the expression of the branching ratios, annihilation cross sections and decay rates in terms of the couplings between DM and SM particles.
elements.sent-2411-498: However, from now on, we adopt a model-independent approach where DM annihilation and decay channels are treated independently, the final states are only SM particles, and $\langle\sigma v\rangle$ and $\Gamma$ are free parameters.
elements.sent-2411-499: This approach is often used in phenomenological studies of DM ID, as it is unbiased and allows for a common ground where results can be easily compared from one study to another.

Taken from~\cite{Arina:2023eic}.} \label{fig:fluxatprod}
elements.sent-2411-500: The spectrum at production of stable particles $dN_i/dK_i$ for a specific channel can be computed using Monte-Carlo simulations, e.g.\ \verb|PYTHIA|~\cite{Bierlich:2022pfr or \verb|HERWIG|~\cite{Bahr:2008pv,Bellm:2019zci , however they require a good understanding of their tunable parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-501: Instead, other numerical codes can provide these spectra, such as \verb|PPPC4DMID|~\cite{Cirelli:2010xx , \verb|Hazma|~\cite{Coogan:2019qpu,Coogan:2022cdd , \verb|HDMSpectra|~\cite{Bauer:2020jay or \verb|CosmiXs|~\cite{Arina:2023eic , that are based on the interpolation of results from well-tuned Monte-Carlo simulations and/or analytical computations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-502: To illustrate, we show in Figure~\ref{fig:fluxatprod the spectra at production of $\bar{p}$, $\gamma$ and $e^+$ computed using \verb|CosmiXs|, for various DM annihilation channels and for $m_DM = 100$ GeV and $100$ TeV, in terms of the energy fraction $x = K/m_DM$.}
elements.sent-2411-503: In the case where DM is composed of PBHs, we can also expect an indirect signal from them.
elements.sent-2411-504: In 1975, Stephen Hawking has predicted that BHs can emit particles thanks to quantum effects at their event horizon~\cite{Hawking:1975vcx .}
elements.sent-2411-505: This process is known as BH evaporation.
elements.sent-2411-506: For a Schwarzschild (electrically neutral and non-spinning) PBH, the emission rate of particles $i$ per unit time and energy through its evaporation is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:evap} \frac{d^2N_i}{dtdK_i} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_\text{d.o.f.} \frac{\Gamma_i(K_i,M)}{e^{K_i/T}\pm 1} \quad (PBH evaporation)\;,\end{equation} where $+$ ($-$) is taken if $i$ is a fermion (boson), $M$ is the PBH mass, $T = 1/(8\pi G M)$ its temperature, and the sum is performed over the degrees of freedom (d.o.f.)\ of the emitted particles (spin, color, helicity and charge multiplicities).
elements.sent-2411-507: The deviation of this spectrum from a black body one is characterised by the grey body factor $\Gamma_i$ that can be computed semi-analytically or using numerical codes e.g., \verb|BlackHawk|~\cite{Arbey:2019mbc,Arbey:2021mbl .}
elements.sent-2411-508: Secondary processes (hadronisation, decay, ...) can then happen, in the same manner as in the case of annihilating or decaying DM particles.
elements.sent-2411-509: In Figure~\ref{fig:PBHevap we show the emission spectra of $e^+$ and $\gamma$ from PBHs with different masses, computed using \verb|BlackHawk| combined with \verb|Hazma| to deal with the secondary processes.}
elements.sent-2411-510: In this DM scenario the unknowns are the fraction of PBHs constituting the total amount of DM in the Universe $f_PBH\equiv \rho_PBH/\rho_DM$ and the mass distribution of PBHs.
