Computed using \verb|BlackHawk+Hazma|.} \label{fig:PBHevap}
elements.sent-2411-511: Now that we have the tools to compute the spectrum of stable particles produced on the spot of particle DM annihilation or decay and PBH evaporation, we can go into the physics of their propagation in the MW.
elements.sent-2411-512: As we previously mentioned, DM-produced charged CRs are a promising indirect signal as they can be antimatter particles that are detectable over a sufficiently low background, since antimatter particles are scarcely produced in astrophysical processes.
elements.sent-2411-513: In order to compute the flux of DM-produced, stable charged CRs at Earth, we need to take into account their transport in the ISM.
elements.sent-2411-515: Spatial diffusion: As the charged CRs propagate, they interact with the Galactic magnetic fields (GMFs).
elements.sent-2411-516: The turbulent component of GMFs induces a random walk of the CRs, well-described by a spatial diffusion term in Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq that involves a diffusion tensor $D_i(K_i,\vec{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-517: Momentum space diffusion: The interactions between charged CRs and the turbulent component of the GMFs can also provoke the diffusion of CRs in their momentum space, possibly causing their stochastic reacceleration.
elements.sent-2411-518: This effect is described by the term involving the momentum diffusion coefficient $D_{pp (K_i,\vec{x )$ in Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq .}}}
elements.sent-2411-519: Energy losses: CRs can interact with various components of the ISM and lose some of their energy in the process.
elements.sent-2411-520: Hadronic CRs ($\overset{\scriptscriptstyle(-) {p , \overset{\scriptscriptstyle(-) {d}$) can lose their energy through the production of pions by colliding with nuclei in the ISM.}}}
elements.sent-2411-521: All charged CRs can also lose their energy by ionising the gas in the ISM and through Coulomb scattering on free $e^-$ in ionised gas.
elements.sent-2411-522: In Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq , energy losses are represented by the term in $\dot{K}$.}
elements.sent-2411-523: They are encoded in the energy loss function $b_loss(K_i,\vec{x )\equiv -\dot{K}$ that can be computed analytically~\cite{Schlickeiser:2002pg,Buch:2015iya,Evoli:2016xgn and given in Appendix~\ref{apx:elosses (only for $e^\pm$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-524: Convection: Stellar winds and supernovae induce a flow of particles averagely directed outwards the Galaxy, called the Galactic wind.
elements.sent-2411-525: This wind, modeled by the velocity $\vec{v _c(\vec{x )$ in Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq , also contributes to the transport of charged CRs by deflecting them.}}}
elements.sent-2411-526: Interactions with the ISM: $p$ and $d$ CRs can collide with nuclei present in the ISM gas, essentially shattering them, in turn producing more $p$ and $d$ CRs.
elements.sent-2411-527: This process is known as spallation.
elements.sent-2411-528: On the other hand, $\bar{p}$ and $\bar{d}$ CRs can annihilate with their matter counterpart present in the ISM gas.
elements.sent-2411-529: Both of these interactions are encoded in the second right-hand-side term of Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq , where $\beta_i$ is the CR velocity in units of $c$, $n_j(\vec{x )$ is the ISM gas density, $\sigma_{ij}$ is the spallation (annihilation) cross section of $p$ and $d$ ($\bar{p}$ and $\bar{d}$) with the ISM gas, and $j \in$ \{HI, HII, H$_2$, He\}.}}
elements.sent-2411-530: Finally, the number of DM-produced CRs injected at a position $\vec{x}$ in the Galaxy per unit time, volume and energy is encoded in the source term $Q_i(K_i,\vec{x )$ of Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq .}}
elements.sent-2411-531: For particle DM, this term is written \begin{equation}\label{eq:source} Q_i (K_i,\vec{x}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \displaystyle \xi\langle \sigma v\rangle \left(\frac{\rho_DM(\vec{x})}{m_DM}\right)^2\frac{dN_i^{ann}}{dK_i} \text{(annihilation)} \displaystyle \quad \Gamma \ \; \left(\frac{\rho_DM(\vec{x})}{m_DM}\right)\; \frac{dN_i^{dec}}{dK_i} \text{(decay)} \end{array} \right.\;,\end{equation} where $\xi = 1/2$ if DM is its own antiparticle and $1/4$ otherwise, $dN_i/dK_i$ is the injection spectrum of particle $i$ per annihilation or decay of DM-produced CRs that can be computed by the tools described in Section~\ref{subsec:particleprod .}}
elements.sent-2411-532: In the case where DM is in part constituted of PBHs we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:sourcePBH} Q_i(K_i,\vec{x}) = f_PBH\rho_DM(\vec{x}) \int \frac{dM}{M}\frac{dN_PBH}{dM}\frac{d^2N_i}{dtdK_i} \quad (PBH evaporation)\;,\end{equation} where $d^2N_i/dtdK_i$ is the emission rate of particles $i$ through the evaporation of a single PBH, as described in Section~\ref{subsec:particleprod , and $dN_PBH/dM$ represents the mass distribution of PBHs in the MW.}
elements.sent-2411-533: The case of PBH evaporation is essentially similar to the one of decaying DM particles, except that the decay rate $\Gamma$ is contained in the emission rate $d^2N_i/dtdK_i$.
elements.sent-2411-534: This is especially true when we consider the PBH mass distribution to be monochromatic ($dN_PBH/dM = \delta(M-M_PBH)$) where \begin{equation}Q_i(K_i,\vec{x}) = f_PBH\left(\frac{\rho_DM(\vec{x})}{M_PBH}\right) \frac{d^2N_i}{dtdK_i} \quad (monochromatic)\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-535: Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq has to be solved within a certain boundary of space.}
elements.sent-2411-536: The different processes that affect the propagation of charged CRs involve ingredients that have a cylindrical symmetry in a first approximation, such as the GMFs, ISM gas and ambient photon densities.
elements.sent-2411-537: It is therefore customary to solve Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq within the boundaries of a cylinder of radius $R_max$ and height $L$ (commonly known as the halo height) that represents the boundaries of the Galaxy~\cite{Taillet:2002ub,Strong:1998pw .}}
elements.sent-2411-538: In other words, $R_max$ and $L$ are implicit components of the propagation equation.
elements.sent-2411-539: Although an analytical solution to the full Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq does not exist, one can make some assumptions to simplify and solve it.}
elements.sent-2411-540: Alternatively, it can be solved numerically as long as the different ingredients involved are parametrised properly.
elements.sent-2411-541: As such, many studies consider the spatial diffusion tensor $D$ to be a rigidity-dependent\footnote{The rigidity is defined as the ratio between the CR momentum and charge: $R \equiv p/\lvert q \rvert$.}
elements.sent-2411-542: The momentum space diffusion coefficient $D_{pp}$ can also be parametrised (assuming the turbulence in the GMFs is characterised by Alfv\'en waves~\cite{Berezinsky:1990qxi,1994ApJ...431..705S ) \begin{equation}\label{eq:momdiffcoef} D_{pp}(p) = \frac{4}{3\delta(4-\delta^2)(4-\delta)}\frac{p^2v_A^2}{D(p)}\;,\end{equation} where $v_A$ is the Alfv\'en velocity.}
elements.sent-2411-543: The value of the propagation parameters involved in Equations~\ref{eq:transporteq , \ref{eq:diffcoef and \ref{eq:momdiffcoef (alongside $R_max$ and $L$) can have a sizeable impact on the CR flux predictions, but they can be constrained by fitting the latter on local fluxes and flux-ratios of different CR species (such as $e^\pm$, $p/\bar{p}$, but also lithium, beryllium, carbon and boron nuclei) measured by the various experiments listed in Section~\ref{subsec:expCCRs .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-544: When it comes to the ISM gas, GMFs and ambient photons maps, a plethora of models is available, all more or less based on observations.
elements.sent-2411-545: Once all of the aforementioned ingredients are carefully set, Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq can then be solved using numerical codes such as \verb|GALPROP|~\cite{2011CoPhC.182.1156V,galpropweb , \verb|DRAGON2|~\cite{Evoli:2016xgn,Evoli:2017vim and \verb|USINE|~\cite{Maurin:2018rmm or semi-analytically.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-546: In the end, we obtain the differential flux of propagated DM-produced CRs of species $i$ at any position $\vec{x}$ in the Galaxy, which is written \begin{equation}\label{eq:CRflux} \frac{d\Phi_i}{dK_i}(K_i,\vec{x}) \equiv \frac{c\beta_i}{4\pi}f_i(K_i,\vec{x})\;,\end{equation} and, when evaluated at Earth's position, can then be directly used to either explain excesses in CR data, allowing us to pinpoint the value of the free parameters we previously mentioned (such as $\langle\sigma v\rangle$, $m_DM$, ...), or otherwise set constraints on them.
elements.sent-2411-547: In this section, we go through the computation of the flux of DM-produced photons and neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-548: These particles can be directly emitted by DM annihilation, decay or PBH evaporation, which are referred to as prompt emissions.
elements.sent-2411-549: In comparison to charged CRs, photons and neutrinos are not deviated by the GMFs and do not interact much with the ISM, therefore their propagation is not described by Equation~\ref{eq:transporteq .}
elements.sent-2411-550: This also means that their source of emission can be easily identified.
elements.sent-2411-551: On the other hand, DM-produced charged CRs can interact with the ISM, producing (more diffuse) secondary emissions of photons.
elements.sent-2411-552: In this section, we specifically investigate the case of secondary photons emitted by DM-produced $e^\pm$ through ICS, synchrotron and bremsstrahlung.

elements.sent-2411-577: Now that we have an idea of what an indirect signal from DM might look like, we need to compare the prediction with observations from experiments.
elements.sent-2411-578: In the next section, we give an overview of the available experiments allowing us to detect a possible indirect signal from DM or, if not, help us set constraints on DM.
elements.sent-2411-579: A serious advantage of indirect searches is that they capitalise on the fact that DM could produce astrophysical-like signals, meaning we can use the plethora of already available experiments in order to conduct these searches in addition to dedicated experiments.
elements.sent-2411-580: In this section, we describe some of the experimental techniques used to detect charged CRs (Section~\ref{subsec:expCCRs ), photons (Section~\ref{subsec:expphotons ) and neutrinos (Section~\ref{subsec:expneutrinos ), as well as list some of the most known old, current and future experiments.}}}
elements.sent-2411-581: Charged CRs can be detected using three main techniques:.
elements.sent-2411-582: When a charged CRs enters Earth's atmosphere, their successive interactions with elements provoke a particle cascade called an air shower.
elements.sent-2411-583: One technique consists of measuring the Cherenkov radiation emitted by shower particles when then travel faster than light in large tanks filled with water.
elements.sent-2411-584: Some of the experiments that use this water Cherenkov technique are {\sc Hawc , {\sc Lhaaso or the Pierre {\sc Auger Observatory, although some of them may also use other complementary detection techniques.}}}
elements.sent-2411-585: Cherenkov radiation can also be emitted in the atmosphere during the particle cascade, and can be probed by so-called imaging atmospheric Cherenkov telescopes such as {\sc Hess , {\sc Magic or {\sc Veritas .}}}
elements.sent-2411-586: Satellite and balloon-borne experiments are also an option.
elements.sent-2411-587: They can collect charged CRs directly and even be equipped with magnetic spectrometers to discriminate their charge and figure out their energy.
elements.sent-2411-588: Such experiments are {\sc Pamela , {\sc Ams-02 or {\sc Dampe .}}}
elements.sent-2411-589: We show the energy range and operation dates of a selection of charged CRs experiments in Figure~\ref{fig:expCRs .}
elements.sent-2411-590: In 2008, the {\sc Pamela experiment have reported an excess in the fraction $e^+/(e^++e^-)$ between $10$ GeV and $100$ GeV~\cite{PAMELA:2008gwm , later confirmed by the {\sc Ams experiment in 2014 and extended up to $300$ GeV~\cite{AMS:2013fma,AMS:2014bun .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-591: This of course encouraged the community to provide a DM explanation of this excess and the most credible candidate was annihilations of leptophilic\footnote{DM has to be leptophilic to produce $e^\pm$ but also to avoid the production of $\bar{p}$, in order to match the absence of a $\bar{p}$ excess.}
elements.sent-2411-592: DM with a mass in the TeV range.
elements.sent-2411-593: However, it was difficult to satisfy both the excess in the $e^+$ fraction and the $e^+$ flux at the same time with a single candidate.
elements.sent-2411-594: Moreover, as the time passed, other ID searches involving measurements of the CMB~\cite{Slatyer:2015jla,Slatyer:2015kla and the isotropic $\gamma$-ray background~\cite{Fermi-LAT:2015qzw have then excluded this possibility.}}
elements.sent-2411-595: Other explanations involving a mismodeling of astrophysical sources seem to be the most probable ones~\cite{Serpico:2011wg .}

elements.sent-2411-613: Currently, space based observatories of $X$- and $\gamma$-rays work using three detection methods that are efficient at different energy ranges:.
elements.sent-2411-614: Photoelectric effect: Energetic enough photons can eject electrons in a material inside the detector, that in turn will get tracked and get its energy measured in a calorimeter.
elements.sent-2411-615: This process, useful for detecting photons up to $\sim 10$ MeV, is used in $X$-ray and soft $\gamma$-ray observatories such as {\sc NuStar , {\sc Xmm-Newton or {\sc Integral .}}}
elements.sent-2411-616: Compton scattering: By measuring the recoil of an electron from an incoming photon, and the energy of the scattered photon, we can deduce the total energy of the incident photon and its direction.
elements.sent-2411-617: This process was used to detect $\sim 1-30$ MeV photons in the {\sc Comptel observatory.}
elements.sent-2411-618: Pair production: Incoming photons produce a $e^+e^-$ pair that gets tracked in the detector and their energy measured in a calorimeter.
elements.sent-2411-619: This process is used in hard $\gamma$-ray experiments such as {\sc Fermi , that can detect photons above $\sim 20$ MeV and up to $500$~GeV.}
elements.sent-2411-620: At the first sight, we would think that the full range of $X$- and harder $\gamma$-rays is equally covered thanks to the overlapping of the detection techniques.
elements.sent-2411-621: However the sensitivity of observatories using the photoelectric effect worsen at higher energies, and the {\sc Comptel observatory, the only one using the Compton scattering technique, was built in the 1990s and therefore was not as sensitive as current observatories.}
elements.sent-2411-622: This results in the so-called MeV gap shown in Figure~\ref{fig:MeVgap : there are no current observatories that are able to probe photons between $\sim 100$ keV and $100$ MeV with a satisfying enough sensitivity.}
elements.sent-2411-623: This can be an issue when trying to probe indirect photon signals from sub-GeV DM, as we will discuss in Chapter~\ref{chap:subGeV .}
elements.sent-2411-624: Fortunately, proposed experiments such as {\sc e-Astrogam and {\sc Amego plan to fill this gap in the next decade.}}

elements.sent-2411-625: Three main excesses have been reported in the $X$- and $\gamma$-ray energy range, the first one being the $\gamma$-ray excess around the GC we discussed thoroughly in Section~\ref{subsec:MW .}

elements.sent-2411-632: Finally, neutrinos can be detected in large underground water or ice Cherenkov detectors.
elements.sent-2411-633: When neutrinos interact with the material in the detector or its surroundings, secondary particles are produced (most of the time muons) which can emit Cherenkov radiations when they go through the material, and be detected.
elements.sent-2411-634: The energy and direction of incident neutrinos can then be reconstructed.
elements.sent-2411-635: We show the energy range and operation dates of a selection of neutrino experiments in the right panel of Figure~\ref{fig:expCRs .}

elements.chapter-2411-006: Constraining sub-GeV dark matter from diffuse \texorpdfstring{$X$}
elements.sent-2411-639: Sub-GeV DM, as we call it in Section~\ref{subsec:candidates , appears as a solid, theoretically well motivated alternative~\cite{Knapen:2017xzo,Boehm:2002yz,Boehm:2003hm,Fayet:2007ua,Boehm:2003bt,Ahn:2005ck,Boehm:2006mi,Ema:2020fit,Hochberg:2014dra,Boddy:2014yra,Hochberg:2014kqa,Choi:2017zww,Berlin:2018tvf,DAgnolo:2015ujb,Falkowski:2011xh,Lin:2011gj,Hooper:2007tu,Bertuzzo:2017lwt,Darme:2017glc,Katz:2020ywn .}}
elements.sent-2411-640: However, detection of this particular DM candidate is more challenging than WIMPs~\cite{Cirelli:2020bpc .}
elements.sent-2411-641: In DM ID, recalling a discussion in Section~\ref{subsec:expphotons , the two main obstacles that prevent us to have high-sensitivity data in the interval where the signals from sub-GeV DM particle annihilation or decay are expected are i) the solar activity, which screens DM-produced $e^\pm$ and ii) the MeV gap in $X$- and soft $\gamma$-ray experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-642: A novel technique introduced in~\cite{Cirelli:2020bpc allows to circumvent both of these problems.}
elements.sent-2411-643: The idea is to focus on secondary emissions from DM, and in particular on the ICS of DM-produced $e^\pm$ on Galactic ambient light, which produces hard $X$-rays with typical keV energies.
elements.sent-2411-644: As a result, one can leverage on the abundant data in $X$-ray keV observations, rather than the scarce MeV experiments, in order to test sub-GeV DM.
elements.sent-2411-645: In \cite{Cirelli:2020bpc it was shown that the method is powerful: using data from a large region of the inner Galaxy observed by the {\sc Integral/Spi spectrometer, the authors were able to obtain stringent constraints on annihilating DM, i.e.\ on $\langle\sigma v\rangle$, in the $m_DM$ range between $1$ MeV and $5$ GeV, even with a conservative propagation setup for DM-produced $e^\pm$.}}
elements.sent-2411-646: This chapter follows up on that work, embarking in a systematic analysis of the available $X$-rays datasets in order to assess their full constraining power on sub-GeV DM, along the lines of the strategy described above.
elements.sent-2411-647: In addition, we consider both the case of annihilating and decaying DM.
elements.sent-2411-648: We start by recalling the formalism and the relevant quantities necessary for computing prompt and secondary $X$-ray emissions from sub-GeV DM annihilations and decays in Section~\ref{sec:XrayDM , in Section~\ref{sec:data we detail the datasets that we use and our analysis, in Section~\ref{sec:results1 the main results, and in Section~\ref{sec:comparison we compare them to related studies.}}}}

elements.chapter-2411-008: Constraining primordial black holes from Galactic emissions
elements.sent-2411-950: To this day, PBHs with a mass between $\sim 5\times10^{17}$ and $\sim 10^{22}$ g remain totally unconstrained, meaning that, in this mass range, there is a possibility that they can constitute the entirety of DM~\cite{Carr:2020gox .}
elements.sent-2411-951: On the lower end of this range, constraints on $f_PBH$ come from PBH evaporation, which consists of a source of indirect signals from PBHs, in the same manner as decaying DM.
elements.sent-2411-952: In the following, we improve these limits by considering three different diffuse probes of PBH evaporation: i) the flux of PBH-evaporated $e^\pm$, ii) the flux of secondary $X$-rays from the propagation of the $e^\pm$ in the ISM and iii) the flux of $511$ keV photons coming from the annihilation of PBH-produced $e^+$ with free $e^-$ in the ISM.
elements.sent-2411-953: For the first two probes, we employ the same computation scheme as in Chapter~\ref{chap:prop , i.e.\ dealing with the propagation of PBH-produced $e^\pm$ using the numerical code \verb|DRAGON2| with the same propagation setup, and computing the secondary emission of $X$-rays from ICS of these $e^\pm$ on Galactic ambient photons using \verb|HERMES|.}
elements.sent-2411-954: This chapter is organised as follows: in Section~\ref{sec:injection we cover the injection of $e^\pm$ from PBH evaporation, in Section~\ref{sec:diffuse we explore the computation of the flux from the three aforementioned diffuse probes and finally in Section~\ref{sec:result+discussion we discuss our main results and compare them with other ones from the literature.}}}
elements.sent-2411-955: Recalling the second part of Section~\ref{subsec:particleprod , Schwarzschild BHs evaporate over time by emitting particles at a rate described by Equation~\ref{eq:evap .}}
elements.sent-2411-956: In this chapter, we however generalise this equation to the case of Kerr (electrically neutral, spinning) BHs, which are characterised by their reduced spin parameter $a^\star \equiv J/(GM^2)$, with $J$ being its angular momentum and $G$ the Newtonian gravitational constant.
elements.sent-2411-957: The temperature of a Kerr BH is written~\cite{Arbey:2019jmj \begin{equation}\label{eq:BHtemp} T = \frac{1}{4\pi GM} \frac{\sqrt{1-{a^\star}^2}}{1+\sqrt{1-{a^\star}^2}}\;,\end{equation} retrieving $T = 1/(8\pi GM)$ for Schwarzschild BHs (i.e.\ $a^\star = 0$).}
elements.sent-2411-958: Equation~\ref{eq:BHtemp also imposes that $a^\star < 1$, since if $a^\star =1$ then $T=0$, which is forbidden by thermodynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-959: Then, the emission spectrum of primary particles $i$ is given by \begin{equation}\frac{d^2N_i}{dtdE_i} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_\text{d.o.f.} \frac{\Gamma_i(E_i,M,a^\star)}{e^{E'_i/T}\pm 1}\;, \label{eq:prim_spec_BH}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}E'_i \equiv E_i - m\Omega \quad and \quad \Omega \equiv \frac{a^\star}{2GM\left(1+\sqrt{1-{a^\star}^2}\right)}\;,\end{equation} are the total energy of the emitted particle and the BH horizon rotational energy, respectively, and $m = \{-l, ..., l\}$ is the projection on the BH axis of the particle angular momentum $l$.

elements.section-2411-020: Upper integration bound for the line of sight coordinate
elements.sent-2411-1160: In this section, we compute the maximum value of the l.o.s.\ coordinate $s$ in the setup where the spatial boundary of the Galaxy is a cylinder of radius $R_max$ and height $L$, useful for Section~\ref{subsec:secondary .}
elements.sent-2411-1161: If the first condition is reached first, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:cond1} R(s_max) = R_max \implies (x(s_max)-r_\odot)^2+y^2(s_max)-R_max = 0 \label{eq:cond1sol} \implies s_max^\pm = \frac{r_\odot f_x \pm r_\odot\sqrt{f_x^2+\left(f_x^2+f_y^2\right)\left[\left(\frac{R_max}{r_\odot}\right)^2-1\right]}}{f_x^2+f_y^2}\;,\end{equation} where $f_i= i/s$ encapsulates the angular dependency of the coordinate $i$.

elements.sent-2411-1171: In the Thomson limit (which was used throughout the thesis), the $e^\pm$ energy losses through ICS are encoded in \begin{equation}b_ICS(E_e,\vec{x}) = \frac{4c\sigma_T}{3m_e^2}E_e^2\int_0^\infty dE_\gamma\, n_\gamma(E_\gamma, \vec{x})\;,\end{equation} where $\sigma_T \equiv 8\pi r_e^2/3 = 6.65\times10^{-25}$ cm$^2$ is the Thomson scattering cross section (where $r_e$ is the $e^\pm$ classical radius), and $n_\gamma(E_\gamma,\vec{x )$ is the ambient photon number density at a position $\vec{x}$ in the Galaxy.}
elements.sent-2411-1172: The integral in this expression represents the total energy density of these photons.
elements.sent-2411-1173: The energy losses of $e^\pm$ through the synchrotron radiation are written \begin{equation}b_syn(E_e,\vec{x}) = \frac{4c\sigma_T}{3m_e^2}E_e^2\frac{B^2(\vec{x})}{8\pi}\;,\end{equation} where $B(\vec{x )$ is the intensity of the GMF at the position $\vec{x}$, summing its continuous and turbulent components.}
elements.sent-2411-1174: This expression is analogous to the ICS energy-loss coefficient, since $B^2/8\pi$ is the total energy density of the GMF.
elements.sent-2411-1175: The energy losses through bremsstrahlung are described by \begin{equation}b_brems(E_e,\vec{x}) = c\sum_i n_i(\vec{x}) \int_0^{E_e}dE_\gamma\, E_\gamma \frac{d\sigma_i}{dE_\gamma}(E_\gamma,E_e)\;,\end{equation} where $n_i(\vec{x )$ is the number density of the gas species $i$ at a position $\vec{x}$ in the Galaxy and $d\sigma_i/dE_\gamma$ is the differential bremsstrahlung cross sections, which are written~\cite{Bethe:1934za \begin{equation}\frac{d\sigma_i}{dE_\gamma}(E_\gamma,E_e) = \frac{3\alpha\sigma_T}{8\pi E_\gamma}\left\{\left[1+\left(1-\frac{E_\gamma}{E_e}\right)^2\right]\phi^i_1(E_e,E_\gamma)-\frac{2}{3}\left(1-\frac{E_\gamma}{E_e}\right)\phi^i_2(E_e,E_\gamma)\right\}\;,\end{equation} where $\phi_{1,2 ^i$ are scattering functions whose expressions are quite cumbersome to formulate, and out of the scope of the thesis.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1176: We however consider two limiting regimes.
elements.sent-2411-1177: When the impinging $e^\pm$ is highly relativistic ($\gamma_e \gtrsim 10^3$), the electronic cloud around the atoms in the gas screens their nucleus, effectively decreasing the bremsstrahlung emission.
elements.sent-2411-1178: The scattering functions are essentially constant in this strong shielding regime.
elements.sent-2411-1179: Their value for HI and HeI are given in Table~2 in~\cite{Blumenthal:1970gc and rewritten here for the convenience of the reader: \begin{equation}\phi_{1,ss}^HI = 45.79\;, \quad \phi_{2,ss}^HI = 44.46\;, \phi_{1,ss}^HeI = 134.60\;, \quad \phi_{2,ss}^HeI = 131.40\;,\end{equation} and the energy losses are then given by \begin{equation}b_brems^neut,ss(E_e,\vec{x}) = \frac{3c\alpha\sigma_T}{8\pi}E_e^2 \sum_i n_i (\vec{x}) \left(\frac{4}{3}\phi_{1,ss}^i-\frac{1}{3}\phi_{2,ss}^i \right)\;.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1180: Otherwise, for less relativistic incoming $e^\pm$ ($\gamma_e \lesssim 10^2$), electronic screening becomes inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-1181: This is referred to as the weak shielding regime.
elements.sent-2411-1182: It essentially corresponds to the case where the gas is ionised.
elements.sent-2411-1183: In this regime, the scattering functions are written \begin{equation}\phi^i_{1,ion+ws}(E_e,E_\gamma) = \phi^i_{2,ion+ws}(E_e,E_\gamma) = 4Z_i(Z_i+1)\left\{\log\left[\frac{2E_e}{m_ec^2}\left(\frac{E_e-E_\gamma}{E_\gamma}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2}\right\}\,,\end{equation} where $Z_i$ is the number of protons in a nucleus of gas species $i$.
elements.sent-2411-1184: When the gas is ionised, these scattering functions are valid for the whole impinging $e^\pm$ energy range, since there is no more electronic screening.
elements.sent-2411-1185: The energy loss are written, in the case the gas is ionised or neutral and weakly shielded \begin{equation}b_brems^ion+ws(E_e,\vec{x}) = \frac{3c\alpha\sigma_T}{2\pi}E_e^2 \left[\log\left(\frac{2E_e}{m_e}\right) -\frac{1}{3}\right]\sum_i n_i (\vec{x}) Z_i(Z_i+1)\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1186: In order to study the intermediate regime between the strong- and weak-shielding ones (i.e.\ $10^2 \lesssim \gamma_e \lesssim 10^3$), we can perform an interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-1187: The energy loss rate of $e^\pm$ through their scattering on free $e^-$ in an ionised plasma is \begin{equation}b_Coul(E_e,\vec{x}) = \frac{3c\sigma_T}{4}n_e(\vec{x})\left[\log\left(\frac{E_e}{m_e}\right)+2\log\left(\frac{m_e}{E_p(\vec{x})}\right)\right]\;,\end{equation} where $n_e(\vec{x )$ is the number density of free $e^-$ at a position $\vec{x}$ in the MW and $E_p \equiv \sqrt{4\pi n_e r_e^3 m_e/\alpha$ is the minimum excitation energy of the plasma.}}
elements.sent-2411-1188: The energy loss function is written in this case \begin{equation}b_ion(E_e,\vec{x}) = \frac{9c\sigma_T}{4}\sum_i n_i(\vec{x})Z_i \left[\log\left(\frac{E_e}{m_e}\right)+\frac{2}{3}\log\left(\frac{m_e}{\Delta E_i}\right)\right]\;,\end{equation} where $\Delta E_i$ is the average excitation energy of the gas species $i$, which is $15.0$ eV for HI and $41.5$~eV for HeI.. \pagestyle{fancy.}
